articles.title: About this site
articles.description: Some additional details about the website
articles.contents:
This website was created as part of the GV300 module to showcase selected pieces of my quantitative work. The aim is to demonstrate how I approach data analysis in R, from cleaning datasets to producing visualisations and interpreting results clearly.
The GV300 Showcase page presents an analysis of electoral participation in the UK using official election data. It highlights how statistical techniques and data visualisation can be used to explore inequality and representation in a meaningful way. All code is embedded directly within the site to ensure transparency and reproducibility.
Overall, this site exhibits the analytical skills I have developed throughout the module, as well as my ability to communicate statistical findings in a clear and accessible manner.

articles.title: GV300 Showcase
articles.description: My favourite quantitative analysis and work
articles.contents:
This section showcases part of my analysis from GV300 Assignment 2, where I examined inequality inconstituency sizes across the UK.
I utilised official election data. Furthermore, I explored how the number of valid votes differs between counties.


library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)




uk_csv <- "https://electionresults.parliament.uk/general-elections/6/candidacies.csv"
uk_raw <- readr::read_csv(uk_csv, show_col_types = FALSE)
names(uk_raw) <- names(uk_raw) %>% tolower() %>% str_replace_all("\\s+", "_")




uk1 <- uk_raw %>%
  filter(!`election_is_by-election`)

uk2 <- uk1 %>%
  select(
    constituency = constituency_name,
    country      = country_name,
    valid_votes  = candidate_vote_count,
    electorate   = electorate
  )

uk3 <- uk2 %>%
  group_by(country, constituency) %>%
  summarise(
    valid_votes = sum(valid_votes, na.rm = TRUE),
    electorate  = max(electorate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

uk <- uk3 %>%
  mutate(turnout_registered = 100 * valid_votes / electorate) %>%
  filter(is.finite(turnout_registered), electorate > 0)




print(head(uk))

# A tibble: 6 × 5
  country constituency       valid_votes electorate turnout_registered
  <chr>   <chr>                    <dbl>      <dbl>              <dbl>
1 England Aldershot                48544      78553               61.8
2 England Aldridge-Brownhil…       40912      70268               58.2
3 England Altrincham and Sa…       51452      74025               69.5
4 England Amber Valley             42531      71546               59.4
5 England Arundel and South…       54740      77969               70.2
6 England Ashfield                 39882      68929               57.9



ggplot(uk, aes(x = valid_votes, fill = country)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Constituency Sizes by Country",
    subtitle = "Density plots show the shape of distributions clearly",
    x = "Number of Valid Votes",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none"
  )



The density plots show how constituency sizes are distributed within each country. Northern Ireland appears more tightly clustered, meaning constituency sizes are relatively similar. In contrast, England shows a much wider spread, indicating greater variation in the number of valid votes per constituency. This variation has implications for political representation, as differences in constituency size affect the principle of equal voter power. Visualising the distributions makes patterns of inequality clearer than summary statistics alone.

articles.title: Blessing Nsita Inno
articles.description:
Personal academic website created for GV300. 
Showing selected political quantitative analysis and data visualisation work.
articles.contents:
Welcome
Hi, I’m Blessing — thank you for visiting my website.
This site brings together selected pieces of work from my GV300 module, where I explored political data using R and statistical modelling. Rather than simply presenting results, the aim is to show how I think through data: from structuring datasets to interpreting patterns and drawing insightful conclusions.
I’m particularly interested in how quantitative methods can be used to understand political behaviour and representation in real-world contexts. The showcase page highlights one example of this approach in practice.
Feel free to explore using the navigation menu above, have fun.
You can view my featured GV300 showcase using the navigation menu above. Hopefully it runs smoothly!

articles.title: Text Analysis: Dictionary Approach
articles.description: A simple dictionary approach to political text
articles.contents:
This section demonstrates a dictionary-based approach to text analysis using short examples of central bank-style communication. The aim is to show how written political and economic language can be transformed into measurable categories such as uncertainty, inflation, and output. This approach is useful because it allows researchers to compare themes across documents in a systematic and reproducible way.


library(quanteda)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)




## Preparing the text

texts <- c(
  "The central bank noted that inflation remains elevated while economic growth shows signs of slowing and uncertainty remains high.",
  "Policymakers emphasised that employment levels are improving, although risks to the economic outlook persist.",
  "The committee highlighted concerns about inflation pressures and adopted a cautious stance given ongoing uncertainty in global markets."
)

txtCorpus <- corpus(texts)
tokensFromCorp <- tokens(txtCorpus)
dfmCorp <- dfm(tokensFromCorp)

topfeatures(dfmCorp, 10)

        the           .        that   inflation     remains 
          3           3           2           2           2 
   economic         and uncertainty     central        bank 
          2           2           2           1           1 

Applying a dictionary


## Applying a dictionary
econ_dict <- dictionary(list(
  uncertainty = c("uncertain", "uncertainty", "risk", "risks", "cautious"),
  inflation = c("inflation", "price", "cost"),
  output = c("growth", "output", "employment")
))

dict_results <- dfm_lookup(dfmCorp, dictionary = econ_dict)
convert(dict_results, to = "data.frame")

  doc_id uncertainty inflation output
1  text1           1         1      1
2  text2           1         0      1
3  text3           2         1      0

Visualising dictionary categories


## Visualising dictionary categories

dict_df <- convert(dict_results, to = "data.frame")

names(dict_df)[1] <- "document"

dict_df_long <- dict_df |>
  pivot_longer(cols = -document, names_to = "category", values_to = "count")

ggplot(dict_df_long, aes(x = category, y = count, fill = category)) +
  geom_col() +
  facet_wrap(~ document) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Dictionary-based text analysis",
    x = "Category",
    y = "Word count"
  )



Interpretation
The results show how often different economic themes appear across the sample texts. In particular, words related to uncertainty, inflation, and output can be grouped and counted, allowing written statements to be analysed more systematically. This highlights one of the key strengths of text analysis: it turns qualitative language into quantitative evidence that can be compared across documents.

Blessing Nsita Inno's Webpage
          
          
          Home
          Postcard
          GV300 Showcase
          About Me
          ☰
          
          
      
        
          
            Blessing-nsita Inno
          
          
            
              I am an undergraduate student studying BSc Economics
              and Politics at the University of Essex. My academic
              interests involve economic theory, political science and
              further expanding my knowledge on how economics link to my
              everyday reality.
              Outside of my studies, I enjoy fitness, exploring new
              restaurants, and travelling anywhere I possibly can. I am
              particularly interested in how data and statistical
              strategies can be used to better understand real-world
              political decision-making.
              This website was created for GV300 and showcases
              selected examples of my code and data visualisations from
              the module.
